content:
Case 2:
Initial presentation:
- Age: 24
- Sex: female
- Chief complaint: New skin changes on the back and extremities
- Vital signs: BP 120/75 mmHg, HR 116/min, T° 36.8 °C, respiratory rate 16/min.
Setting: Outpatient/Day Unit (Initial Presentation)
Capabilities: You are evaluating a clinically stable patient with new symptoms in an ambulatory or day-unit setting. You can perform a focused history and examination, obtain routine blood panels, and arrange standard non-invasive imaging as first-line tests. Invasive tissue sampling and specialized analyses usually require additional coordination and are not available in this round.
Provide either a request for a single information item or solve.

{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "focused physical examination (detailed description of skin lesions: morphology, distribution, size, color, blanching/nonblanching, presence of scale/crust/ulceration; check for lymphadenopathy, mucosal lesions, and hepatosplenomegaly)"}}

response.request: focused physical examination (detailed description of skin lesions: morphology, distribution, size, color, blanching/nonblanching, presence of scale/crust/ulceration; check for lymphadenopathy, mucosal lesions, and hepatosplenomegaly)

content:
History Physical:
On June 4, 2025, the patient presented with new skin changes, first noticed by her partner on the back and now also on the extremities. She reported intermittent headaches and mild nausea, as well as chronic joint pain and stiffness. Otherwise, she was in good general condition, afebrile, and showed no signs of skin or mucosal GvHD. A marked psychological stress reaction was noted.
Current medication: pantoprazole 40 mg 1-0-0, vitamin D 20,000 IE 1×/week, calcium 500 mg 1-0-0, posaconazole 300 mg 1×/d, ruxolitinib 10 mg 1-0-1, valaciclovir 500 mg 1-0-1, co-trimoxazole 960 mg orally once daily, three times per week (Monday, Wednesday, Friday), steroid cream p.r.n.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "complete blood count with differential"}}

Cbc:
CBC: Hb 14.2 g/dL, WBC 6.8 ×10⁹/L, Plt 240 ×10⁹/L; MCV 89 fL, MCH 30 pg; differential: neutrophils 58%, lymphocytes 32%, monocytes 7%, eosinophils 2%, basophils 1%.

response.request: detailed dermatologic examination: precise morphology of lesions (macules/papules/vesicles/pustules/plaques/petechiae), size, color, blanching vs nonblanching, presence of scale/crust/ulceration or excoriation, distribution/pattern, associated pruritus or pain, and exam for regional lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly

solve.working_diagnosis: Probable drug-induced morbilliform eruption (most likely TMP-SMX or other recent drug) in an immunosuppressed post-transplant patient
differentials: Disseminated viral exanthem (varicella-zoster virus or HSV)
Leukemia cutis (less likely given normal counts)

treatment_plan.action: Immediate dermatology consultation and punch biopsy of a representative lesion for histopathology
Obtain lesion swab for VZV/HSV PCR (and bacterial culture) to evaluate for viral or infective etiology
Hold co-trimoxazole (TMP-SMX) and any other nonessential recent/new medications suspected to cause rash pending evaluation
Initiate symptomatic treatment with high-potency topical corticosteroid to affected areas and oral antihistamine for pruritus

If lesions progress (vesiculation, mucosal involvement), systemic signs develop, or PCR/biopsy indicate viral dissemination or severe drug reaction, admit and start IV acyclovir and coordinate with dermatology and infectious diseases for systemic immunomodulatory therapy (e.g., systemic steroids) as indicated

content: {"response": {"action": "request", "request": "punch skin biopsy with histopathology report (H&E) and available immunohistochemistry results from a representative lesion"}}

response.request: punch skin biopsy with histopathology report (H&E) and available immunohistochemistry results from a representative lesion

content:
Skin Biopsy:
Skin biopsy (2025-06-07): The dermis and subcutis are diffusely infiltrated by atypical hematopoietic cells in a perivascular and interstitial distribution. The cells show irregular nuclear contours, vesicular chromatin, and occasional nucleoli. Mitotic figures are frequent. Immunohistochemistry: CD34-, CD33+, MPO+, Lysozyme and CD68+, CD117+, negative for CD20-, CD19-, CD3-. Molecular pathology: NPM1 mutation present in cutaneous infiltration.

content:
'bone marrow aspirate and biopsy with morphology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics (karyotype/FISH) and molecular panel including NPM1 and FLT3' not available. Try 'bone_marrow' instead.
You requested multiple marrow studies. Bone marrow morphology, marrow flow cytometry, and conventional cytogenetics/karyotype are available in this round; the comprehensive bone-marrow molecular/NGS panel (including NPM1/FLT3) is only released in a later round. Consider requesting the individual available items (bone_marrow, bone_marrow_flow, cytogenetics) now and the molecular panel in the next round.

content:
Bone Marrow:
June 5, 2025: Morphology: Normocellular bone marrow with age-appropriate cellularity. Trilineage hematopoiesis present with some dysmorphic maturation in erythroid, myeloid, and megakaryocytic lineages, consistent with reactive changes after alloSCT. No excess blasts or abnormal infiltrates were observed.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "bone_marrow_flow (flow cytometry of bone marrow aspirate to assess for abnormal myeloid blasts/phenotype and MRD) "}}

response.request: bone_marrow_flow (flow cytometry of bone marrow aspirate to assess for abnormal myeloid blasts/phenotype and MRD)

content:
Bone Marrow Flow:
Flow cytometry: Normal distribution of myeloid cells, mostly immature B cells, with an inverse CD4/CD8 T-cell ratio and normal NK-cell populations. No aberrant antigen expression or increased blast population detected.

FDG-PET/CT (2025-06-07): Revealed multiple FDG-avid cutaneous lesions (SUVmax 6.2) and intra-abdominal lesions adjacent to the left ureter (SUVmax 8.1), accompanied by several mildly FDG-avid mediastinal lymph nodes (SUVmax 4.5).

solve.working_diagnosis: Isolated extramedullary relapse of acute myeloid leukemia (myeloid sarcoma) with NPM1‑mutated cutaneous involvement and PET‑avid intra‑abdominal disease following allogeneic SCT
differentials: Infectious/inflammatory cutaneous process with secondary myeloid reactivity (less likely given IHC and NPM1 mutation)
Chronic graft‑versus‑host disease with atypical infiltrate (less likely given myeloid immunophenotype and MPO positivity)

treatment_plan.action: Obtain targeted molecular testing: quantitative PCR/NGS for NPM1 (and FLT3-ITD/TKD) on peripheral blood and bone marrow to confirm clonality and establish MRD baseline
Perform donor/host chimerism analysis (STR) on peripheral blood and bone marrow to assess engraftment and evaluate for donor‑cell leukemia

Obtain image‑guided/core biopsy of the intra‑abdominal FDG‑avid lesion (if feasible) for histology, IHC and molecular testing to document extracutaneous involvement and compare molecular profile with skin lesion
Convene urgent multidisciplinary meeting (hematology/ transplant, radiation oncology, pathology, infectious diseases, dermatology) to integrate results and formulate definitive therapy
If confirmed isolated extramedullary relapse, consider local radiotherapy to symptomatic/ bulky lesions (skin and abdominal mass) for local control

Consider immunologic approaches: cautiously reduce/hold nonessential immunosuppression (discussion with transplant team) and evaluate eligibility for donor lymphocyte infusion (DLI) if marrow remains negative and GVHD risk acceptable

Initiate systemic anti‑leukemic therapy tailored to post‑transplant relapse (options to discuss: hypomethylating agent ± venetoclax, targeted therapy if actionable mutations identified, or re‑induction chemotherapy) based on performance status and transplant team recommendation

Supportive care: review/optimize antimicrobial prophylaxis, topical therapy and symptomatic management of skin lesions (wound care, analgesia, antihistamines as needed), and arrange close inpatient monitoring for progression or systemic involvement

solve.working_diagnosis: Isolated extramedullary relapse of acute myeloid leukemia (myeloid sarcoma), NPM1‑mutated, following allogeneic stem cell transplantation with morphologically and flow‑negative bone marrow at present
differentials: Occult systemic marrow relapse (molecular MRD without morphologic blasts)
Infectious or inflammatory cutaneous/intra‑abdominal process mimicking myeloid infiltration (less likely given MPO+/CD33+ IHC and NPM1 mutation in lesion)
Atypical chronic graft‑versus‑host or drug reaction with reactive myeloid infiltrate (unlikely given clonal NPM1 mutation)

treatment_plan.action: Obtain quantitative NPM1 molecular testing (qPCR/NGS) on peripheral blood and bone marrow to confirm clonal disease burden and establish an MRD baseline
Perform donor/recipient chimerism analysis (STR) on peripheral blood and, if feasible, on CD34+ or marrow fraction to determine origin of the leukemic clone
Obtain image‑guided/core biopsy of the intra‑abdominal FDG‑avid lesion with histology, IHC and molecular testing to document extracutaneous involvement and compare mutation profile to the skin lesion

Convene an urgent multidisciplinary meeting (hematology/transplant, radiation oncology, pathology, infectious diseases, dermatology) to integrate diagnostic results and finalize a coordinated management plan
Deliver involved‑field radiotherapy to symptomatic or bulky extramedullary lesions (skin and intra‑abdominal mass) for rapid local control while systemic strategy is defined
After discussion with the transplant team, reduce/hold nonessential immunosuppression (e.g., taper ruxolitinib if safe) to enhance graft‑versus‑leukemia effect, balancing GVHD risk
If marrow remains negative and patient has acceptable risk for GVHD, consider donor lymphocyte infusion (DLI) as an immunologic consolidation strategy for isolated extramedullary relapse

Initiate systemic anti‑leukemic therapy tailored to mutation profile and patient fitness: if actionable mutations (e.g., FLT3) are present, use appropriate targeted agent ± HMA/chemo; if no targetable lesion, consider azacitidine + venetoclax or other salvage regimen as bridge to immune intervention or further transplant discussion

Optimize supportive care: review/adjust antimicrobial prophylaxis, provide wound/skin care and topical agents for lesions, analgesia and antipruritic therapy, and monitor for systemic symptoms or progression
Arrange short‑interval response assessments: serial NPM1 qPCR monitoring and repeat PET‑CT or targeted imaging to document treatment response and guide next steps
